content:
# Leviticus 17 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Leviticus 17 (16 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Agents:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic)
**Total disagreements:** 38

---

## Summary

Leviticus 17 is a theologically dense 16-verse chapter with substantial disagreement across all three agents. The primary fault lines are: (1) the juridical formula *dam yechashev* (v. 4), where agents split between "imputed," "reckoned," and "guilty"; (2) the technical cultic vocabulary (*zaraq*, *reyach nichoach*, *shelamim*), where PNEUMA consistently chooses dynamic alternatives; (3) the *se'irim* clause and the *zonim* metaphor (v. 7); (4) the translation philosophy for the blood-life formula in vv. 11 and 14; (5) the rendering of *nevalah* and *treifah* (v. 15); and (6) the persona of address formulas (*ish ish*, *ger*, *ezrach*) where PNEUMA systematically modernizes. The most consequential disagreements are on *dam yechashev* (v. 4), *zaraq* (v. 6), *reyach nichoach* (v. 6), *va-ani* in v. 11, and *nasa' avono* (v. 16). PNEUMA also introduces structural compressions (merging vv. 1–2; omitting clauses in v. 4) that require ruling against.

---

## Disagreement 1 — V. 1: Name form for Moses

**Verse:** 1
**Hebrew:** אֶל-מֹשֶׁה (*el-Moshe*)
**EMET:** "Moshe"
**LOGOS:** "Moses"
**PNEUMA:** "Moses"
**Ruling:** "Moses"
**Type:** Name convention
**Notes:** EMET maintains Hebrew transliteration throughout its literal register. LOGOS and PNEUMA use the established English form. Project convention specifies English name forms in the consensus text throughout.

---

## Disagreement 2 — V. 2: Structural compression — PNEUMA merges vv. 1–2

**Verse:** 1–2
**EMET:** Maintains distinct vv. 1 and 2 with full formulas
**LOGOS:** Maintains distinct vv. 1 and 2 with full formulas
**PNEUMA:** Merges vv. 1–2 into a single sentence; collapses the two-level speech structure (God to Moses; Moses to Israel) into a single voice
**Ruling:** Retain separate verses with full speech structure
**Type:** Structural
**Notes:** The MT has a two-level speech: God speaks to Moses (v. 1), then Moses is instructed to speak to Aaron, his sons, and all the sons of Israel (v. 2). PNEUMA's compression ("The LORD spoke to Moses: 'Speak to Aaron and his sons and to all the Israelites, and give them this command'") eliminates the intermediate address layer. The two-level structure is intentional in the Hebrew and is retained.

---

## Disagreement 3 — V. 2: *bney Yisra'el* — "sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites"

**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (*bney Yisra'el*)
**EMET:** "sons of Yisra'el"
**LOGOS:** "sons of Israel"
**PNEUMA:** "all the Israelites"
**Ruling:** "sons of Israel"
**Type:** Address formula / translation philosophy
**Notes:** PNEUMA's "Israelites" removes the generational-familial resonance of *bney* (the children/descendants of Israel-the-patriarch). Project convention, established from Genesis onward and confirmed at Lev 12:2, retains "sons of Israel" as the formal address formula for *bney Yisra'el*. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows them.

---

## Disagreement 4 — V. 2: *davar* — "word" vs. "thing" vs. paraphrase

**Verse:** 2
**Hebrew:** זֶה הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר-צִוָּה יְהוָה (*zeh ha-davar asher-tzivah YHWH*)
**EMET:** "This is the thing which the LORD has commanded"
**LOGOS:** "This is the word that the LORD has commanded"
**PNEUMA:** "this is what the LORD has ordered"
**Ruling:** "This is the word that the LORD has commanded"
**Type:** Word choice / formal formula
**Notes:** *Davar* in this promulgation formula functions primarily as "word/utterance" rather than "thing/matter." LOGOS's "word" captures the speech-act dimension of the formula. EMET's "thing" is also defensible (*davar* = matter/affair) but slightly weaker for a promulgation context. PNEUMA's "what the LORD has ordered" dissolves the formal *zeh ha-davar* ("this is the word") formula entirely and introduces "ordered" for *tzivah* ("commanded") — an unnecessary departure.

---

## Disagreement 5 — V. 3: *shor* — "ox" vs. "bull"

**Verse:** 3
**Hebrew:** שׁוֹר (*shor*)
**EMET:** "bull"
**LOGOS:** "ox"
**PNEUMA:** "ox"
**Ruling:** "ox"
**Type:** Word choice / livestock terminology
**Notes:** *Shor* is the general term for bovine livestock (either sex). "Bull" (EMET) implies specifically an intact male bovine. "Ox" is the standard rendering for *shor* in the sacrificial code and is not limited to the castrated male. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; ruling follows them.

---

## Disagreement 6 — V. 3: *kesev* — "lamb" vs. "sheep"

**Verse:** 3
**Hebrew:** כֶּשֶׂב (*kesev*)
**EMET:** "lamb"
**LOGOS:** "lamb"
**PNEUMA:** "sheep"
**Ruling:** "lamb"
**Type:** Word choice / livestock terminology
**Notes:** *Kesev* designates the ovine animal in its sacrificial form, most precisely a young sheep (lamb). PNEUMA's "sheep" is too general — it does not distinguish age-grade. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows them.

---

## Disagreement 7 — V. 3: "Any man of the house of Israel" vs. "Any Israelite"

**Verse:** 3
**Hebrew:** אִישׁ אִישׁ מִבֵּית יִשְׂרָאֵל (*ish ish mi-beit Yisra'el*)
**EMET:** "Any man from the house of Yisra'el"
**LOGOS:** "Any man of the house of Israel"
**PNEUMA:** "Any Israelite"
**Ruling:** "Any man of the house of Israel"
**Type:** Legal formula / distributive idiom
**Notes:** The doubled *ish ish* ("a man, a man") is a distributive Hebrew idiom meaning "any man whatsoever, every single man." PNEUMA's "Any Israelite" eliminates both the distributive formula and the corporate designation *beit Yisra'el* ("house of Israel"). EMET and LOGOS agree on the legal form; the ruling preserves the formula. (Note: EMET uses "from the house," LOGOS uses "of the house" — the preposition *mi-* is partitive "from among the house"; "of" is also acceptable and follows LOGOS.)

---

## Disagreement 8 — V. 4: *dam yechashev* — "bloodguilt reckoned" vs. "blood shall be imputed" vs. "guilty of bloodshed"

**Verse:** 4
**Hebrew:** דָּם יֵחָשֵׁב לָאִישׁ הַהוּא (*dam yechashev la-ish ha-hu'*)
**EMET:** "blood shall be imputed to that man"
**LOGOS:** "bloodguilt shall be reckoned to that man"
**PNEUMA:** "that man is guilty of bloodshed"
**Ruling:** "bloodguilt shall be reckoned to that man"
**Type:** Theological-juridical crux / key term
**Notes:** *Dam* ("blood") + *yechashev* (niphal of *chashav*, "to reckon/impute") creates a forensic declaration: the unauthorized field-slaughter is legally "reckoned" as bloodguilt — equivalent to homicide. LOGOS's "bloodguilt shall be reckoned" is the most precise: "bloodguilt" captures the compound crime (blood + guilt) and "reckoned" preserves the technical *chashav* verb. EMET's "imputed" is accurate (the niphal of *chashav* can be rendered "imputed") but "bloodguilt" as the object is clearer than bare "blood." PNEUMA's "guilty of bloodshed" is a free rendering that loses the specific forensic vocabulary of *chashav* — the verb that Paul will employ in Romans 4 (as the LXX's *logizomai*) for the imputation of righteousness. Ruling: LOGOS.

---

## Disagreement 9 — V. 4: PNEUMA omits "before the tabernacle of the LORD"

**Verse:** 4
**Hebrew:** לִפְנֵי מִשְׁכַּן יְהוָה (*lifnei mishkan YHWH*)
**EMET:** "before the tabernacle of the LORD" (included)
**LOGOS:** "before the tabernacle of the LORD" (included)
**PNEUMA:** omitted entirely
**Ruling:** Include "before the tabernacle of the LORD"
**Type:** Completeness / omission
**Notes:** PNEUMA's rendering of v. 4 collapses the full conditional clause significantly, omitting *lifnei mishkan YHWH*. The MT has: (condition) "has not brought it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it as an offering to the LORD before the tabernacle of the LORD." The phrase *lifnei mishkan YHWH* is not redundant — it specifies the spatial relationship: the tent entrance is *before* (in front of) the tabernacle proper. EMET and LOGOS include it; the consensus retains it.

---

## Disagreement 10 — V. 5: "open field" vs. "open country"

**Verse:** 5
**Hebrew:** עַל-פְּנֵי הַשָּׂדֶה (*al-penei ha-sadeh*)
**EMET:** "upon the face of the open field"
**LOGOS:** "in the open field"
**PNEUMA:** "in the open country"
**Ruling:** "in the open field"
**Type:** Word choice
**Notes:** *Ha-sadeh* = "the field, the open land." "Open field" (EMET/LOGOS) is more precise than "open country" (PNEUMA), which suggests a larger, more indefinite territory. EMET's "upon the face of" is literal (*al-penei*) but cumbersome; LOGOS's "in the open field" is clean and accurate. Ruling follows LOGOS.

---

## Disagreement 11 — V. 5: *shelamim* — "peace offerings" vs. "fellowship offerings"

**Verse:** 5
**Hebrew:** זִבְחֵי שְׁלָמִים (*zivchei shelamim*)
**EMET:** "peace offerings"
**LOGOS:** "peace offerings"
**PNEUMA:** "fellowship offerings"
**Ruling:** "peace offerings"
**Type:** Technical term / terminology ruling
**Notes:** The project terminology ruling specifies *shelamim* = "peace offerings." PNEUMA's "fellowship offerings" (the NIV rendering, emphasizing the communal dimension) is not the project standard. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows project convention and prior SOPHIA rulings throughout Leviticus 1–16.

---

## Disagreement 12 — V. 6: *zaraq* — "dash" vs. "throw" vs. "splash"

**Verse:** 6
**Hebrew:** וְזָרַק הַכֹּהֵן (*ve-zaraq ha-kohen*)
**EMET:** "shall dash"
**LOGOS:** "shall throw"
**PNEUMA:** "shall splash"
**Ruling:** "shall dash"
**Type:** Technical cultic term
**Notes:** *Zaraq* is the technical term for the directed throwing of blood from a bowl against the altar sides or base — a distinct action from *hizzah* (sprinkling finely). "Dash" captures both the force and the directed character of the action. "Throw" (LOGOS) is too casual and non-technical. "Splash" (PNEUMA) implies accidental or incidental dispersal, not the deliberate ritual act. "Dash" is the established rendering for *zaraq* in this translation series (cf. Lev 1:5, 11; 3:2, 8, 13). Ruling follows EMET.

---

## Disagreement 13 — V. 6: *reyach nichoach* — "soothing aroma" vs. "pleasing aroma"

**Verse:** 6
**Hebrew:** לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (*le-reyach nichoach*)
**EMET:** "soothing aroma"
**LOGOS:** "pleasing aroma"
**PNEUMA:** "pleasing aroma"
**Ruling:** "soothing aroma"
**Type:** Technical cultic formula / word choice
**Notes:** *Nichoach* derives from *nuach* ("to rest, to settle, to be at ease") — the image is of a smell that causes YHWH to "rest" or "be appeased." "Soothing" preserves this specific semantic root. "Pleasing" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is a generic positive evaluation that does not capture the specific *nuach*-based sense of settling/calming. The formula *reyach nichoach la-YHWH* appears over 40 times in Leviticus; "soothing aroma" has been the consistent SOPHIA ruling throughout Leviticus 1–16 and is maintained.

---

## Disagreement 14 — V. 7: *zonim* — "going a-whoring" vs. "playing the harlot" vs. "prostituting themselves"

**Verse:** 7
**Hebrew:** אֲשֶׁר הֵם זֹנִים אַחֲרֵיהֶם (*asher hem zonim achareihem*)
**EMET:** "after whom they are going a-whoring"
**LOGOS:** "after whom they are playing the harlot"
**PNEUMA:** "the spirits to whom they have been prostituting themselves"
**Ruling:** "after whom they are going a-whoring"
**Type:** Word choice / metaphor
**Notes:** The *qal* participle *zonim* with *achareihem* ("after them") is the standard prophetic covenant-apostasy metaphor expressed as sexual faithlessness. EMET's "going a-whoring" is the most direct and captures both the continuous aspect of the participle and the strong register of the root. LOGOS's "playing the harlot" is a softer formulation. PNEUMA introduces "spirits" (an interpretive addition not in the Hebrew at this point) and rewrites the syntax. EMET's rendering is the most faithful to the Hebrew construction *zonim achareihem* (literally: "committing-fornication after-them"). Note: PNEUMA adds "the spirits" as an interpretive gloss into the translation text itself (not merely in notes), which is an insertion not justified by the Hebrew.

---

## Disagreement 15 — V. 7: *chuqqat olam* — "perpetual statute" vs. "statute forever" vs. "permanent statute"

**Verse:** 7
**Hebrew:** חֻקַּת עוֹלָם (*chuqqat olam*)
**EMET:** "a perpetual statute"
**LOGOS:** "a statute forever"
**PNEUMA:** "a permanent statute"
**Ruling:** "a perpetual statute"
**Type:** Technical formula
**Notes:** *Chuqqat olam* = "statute of eternity." EMET's "perpetual statute" is compact and precise — "perpetual" (from Latin *perpetuus*, "continuous throughout all time") captures *olam* better than "forever" (LOGOS) or "permanent" (PNEUMA). "Permanent" only means "without intended end," whereas *olam* denotes an unending duration. "A statute forever" (LOGOS) is accurate but the postpositional adverb is less idiomatic in English. Ruling follows EMET; consistent with prior SOPHIA rulings on *chuqqat olam* throughout Leviticus.

---

## Disagreement 16 — V. 8: "sojourners" vs. "foreigners living among you"

**Verse:** 8
**Hebrew:** מִן-הַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכָם (*min-ha-ger ha-gar be-tokham*)
**EMET:** "from the sojourner who sojourns among them"
**LOGOS:** "of the sojourners who sojourn among them"
**PNEUMA:** "any foreigner living among you"
**Ruling:** "of the sojourners who sojourn among them"
**Type:** Technical term / consistency
**Notes:** *Ha-ger ha-gar* ("the sojourner who sojourns") is a cognate verbal construction that EMET and LOGOS both preserve. PNEUMA's "foreigner living among you" loses both the technical term *ger* and the cognate verbal construction. The *ger* is a specific legal category — a resident alien integrated into the Israelite community (distinct from a slave, a visitor, or a stranger) — that recurs in vv. 10, 12, 13, 15 and throughout the Holiness Code. "Sojourner" is the consistent project rendering and is required for legal precision. LOGOS's rendering is preferred; EMET's is materially identical.

---

## Disagreement 17 — V. 9: "that man" vs. "that person"

**Verse:** 9
**Hebrew:** הָאִישׁ הַהוּא (*ha-ish ha-hu'*)
**EMET:** "that man"
**LOGOS:** "that man"
**PNEUMA:** "that person"
**Ruling:** "that man"
**Type:** Legal formula / gender
**Notes:** *Ha-ish ha-hu'* = "that man" — *ish* is the masculine singular. The distributive *ish ish* formula used throughout this chapter (vv. 3, 8, 10, 13) and the verdict formula *ha-ish ha-hu'* (vv. 4, 9) are formal legal idioms using the masculine singular. The consensus retains the formal equivalence. PNEUMA's gender-neutral "that person" is a modernizing accommodation not required by the Hebrew. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows them.

---

## Disagreement 18 — V. 10: "cut him off" vs. "cut them off"

**Verse:** 10
**Hebrew:** וְהִכְרַתִּי אֹתוֹ (*ve-hikhratti oto*)
**EMET:** "will cut him off"
**LOGOS:** "will cut him off"
**PNEUMA:** "will cut them off"
**Ruling:** "will cut him off"
**Type:** Gender / number
**Notes:** The Hebrew suffix is masculine singular *oto* ("him"), referring to the individual person eating blood. PNEUMA introduces a plural "them" — a gender-neutral accommodation. This is not warranted by the Hebrew. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows them.

---

## Disagreement 19 — V. 11: "upon the altar" vs. "on the altar"

**Verse:** 11
**Hebrew:** עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (*al-ha-mizbeath*)
**EMET:** "upon the altar"
**LOGOS:** "on the altar"
**PNEUMA:** "on the altar"
**Ruling:** "upon the altar"
**Type:** Preposition / register
**Notes:** *Al* is the Hebrew preposition "upon/over," designating placement on the top or surface of the altar. "Upon" better preserves the spatial directional sense of *al* and is consistent with the project's treatment of *al* + altar constructions throughout Leviticus 1–16. LOGOS and PNEUMA's "on" is acceptable but slightly less precise.

---

## Disagreement 20 — V. 11: Emphasis on "I myself" vs. "I"

**Verse:** 11
**Hebrew:** וַאֲנִי נְתַתִּיו (*va-ani netattiv*)
**EMET:** "and I have given it" (does not mark the emphasis specially)
**LOGOS:** "and I have given it to you"
**PNEUMA:** "I myself have given it to you"
**Ruling:** "I myself have given it to you"
**Type:** Emphasis / grammatical nuance
**Notes:** *Va-ani* fronts the personal pronoun *ani* ("I") in a context where the verb already carries a first-person inflection — this is emphatic, not grammatically required. The emphatic "I" signals theological significance: God is the donor of the atoning blood, not the recipient of a human initiative. PNEUMA correctly marks this with "I myself." LOGOS includes the correct content but does not specially mark the emphasis. EMET's translation notes acknowledge the emphasis but the translation text does not convey it. Ruling follows PNEUMA on this specific emphasis.

---

## Disagreement 21 — V. 11: "for your lives" vs. "for your souls"

**Verse:** 11
**Hebrew:** עַל-נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם (*al-nafshoteikhem*)
**EMET:** "for your lives"
**LOGOS:** "for your lives"
**PNEUMA:** "for your lives"
**Ruling:** "for your lives"
**Type:** Key term (consensus)
**Notes:** All three agents agree: *nafshoteikhem* = "your lives" (not "your souls" — the Platonic-dualist rendering would import anachronistic connotations). The agreement is noted; this is not a substantive disagreement, merely a confirmation of consensus.

---

## Disagreement 22 — V. 11: "by the life" vs. "because it carries life"

**Verse:** 11
**Hebrew:** כִּי-הַדָּם הוּא בַּנֶּפֶשׁ יְכַפֵּר (*ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhapper*)
**EMET:** "for it is the blood that makes atonement by means of the life"
**LOGOS:** "for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life"
**PNEUMA:** "It is the blood — because it carries life — that makes atonement"
**Ruling:** "for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life [*ba-nefesh*]"
**Type:** Theological crux / syntactic
**Notes:** *Ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhapper*: the *be-* prefix on *nefesh* is instrumental ("by/through/by means of") — the blood atones *by virtue of the nefesh it contains.* EMET's "by means of the life" is slightly verbose but precise. LOGOS's "by the life" is clean and accurate. PNEUMA's "because it carries life" is a paraphrase that explains rather than translates — "because it carries life" is an interpretation of *ba-nefesh*, not a rendering of it. The Hebrew's compact instrumental *ba-nefesh* is preserved in the consensus with the bracketed Hebrew term to signal the key word to readers.

---

## Disagreement 23 — V. 12: "No person" vs. "No one"

**Verse:** 12
**Hebrew:** כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ מִכֶּם (*kol-nefesh mi-kem*)
**EMET:** "No person among you"
**LOGOS:** "No person among you"
**PNEUMA:** "No one among you"
**Ruling:** "No person among you"
**Type:** Key term / *nefesh* continuity
**Notes:** *Kol-nefesh* = "any/every *nefesh*," i.e., any living person. The use of *nefesh* as the subject of the prohibition in v. 12 is deliberate: the same word that means "life" in vv. 11 and 14 here designates the individual person. "No person" preserves this connection and the *nefesh* continuity. "No one" (PNEUMA) is idiomatic but dissolves the *nefesh* thread. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows them.

---

## Disagreement 24 — V. 12: Structure — "and the sojourner... shall not eat blood"

**Verse:** 12
**Hebrew:** וְהַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכְכֶם לֹא-יֹאכַל דָּם (*ve-ha-ger ha-gar be-tokhekem lo' yokhal dam*)
**EMET:** "and the sojourner who sojourns among you shall not eat blood"
**LOGOS:** "nor shall any sojourner who sojourn among you eat blood"
**PNEUMA:** "No foreigner living among you shall eat blood"
**Ruling:** "and the sojourner who sojourns among you shall not eat blood"
**Type:** Structural / word choice
**Notes:** The MT structures this as two distinct prohibitions: (a) no Israelite person shall eat blood (v. 12a), and (b) the sojourner who sojourns among you shall not eat blood (v. 12b). The two clauses are coordinate, not subordinate. LOGOS's "nor shall any sojourner" uses a subordinating "nor" that makes the sojourner-clause into a negative addendum. EMET's and PNEUMA's "and the sojourner" preserve the coordinate structure. PNEUMA's "foreigner" is rejected per Disagreement 16. Ruling: EMET's structure with LOGOS's English polish.

---

## Disagreement 25 — V. 13: "wild animal" vs. "beast" vs. "animal"

**Verse:** 13
**Hebrew:** חַיָּה (*chayah*)
**EMET:** "a wild animal"
**LOGOS:** "beast"
**PNEUMA:** "an animal"
**Ruling:** "a wild animal"
**Type:** Technical term / precision
**Notes:** *Chayah* is the specific category of wild animals (as distinct from *beheimah*, domesticated livestock). The distinction is critical to this verse: hunted *chayah* cannot be brought to the altar for blood-rite disposal; therefore the blood is poured and covered instead. LOGOS's "beast" is archaic; PNEUMA's "animal" is too general and loses the wild/domestic distinction that drives the regulation. EMET's "wild animal" is precise and is the ruling.

---

## Disagreement 26 — V. 13: *afar* — "dust" vs. "dirt"

**Verse:** 13
**Hebrew:** בֶּעָפָר (*be-afar*)
**EMET:** "with dust"
**LOGOS:** "with dust"
**PNEUMA:** "with dirt"
**Ruling:** "with dust"
**Type:** Word choice / theological resonance
**Notes:** *Afar* = "dust, loose earth, soil" — the same word in Gen 2:7 (Adam formed from *afar*) and Gen 3:19 ("you are dust and to dust you shall return"). "Dust" preserves the cosmological resonance: the blood-*nefesh* of the animal is returned to the earth from which life came. "Dirt" (PNEUMA) is casual and loses the *afar* echo. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows them.

---

## Disagreement 27 — V. 14: "I have said" vs. "I said" vs. "And so I said"

**Verse:** 14
**Hebrew:** וָאֹמַר (*va-omar*)
**EMET:** "and I said"
**LOGOS:** "I have said"
**PNEUMA:** "And so I said"
**Ruling:** "I have said"
**Type:** Verbal aspect / tense
**Notes:** The waw-consecutive *va-omar* introduces a past divine declaration with ongoing force. LOGOS's "I have said" (English perfect) best conveys both the past origination and the continuing binding force of the decree. EMET's "and I said" (simple past) is accurate but loses the ongoing-force nuance. PNEUMA's "And so I said" introduces a consequential "so" that is interpretive rather than translational. Ruling follows LOGOS.

---

## Disagreement 28 — V. 14: Repetition of the blood-life formula — PNEUMA compresses

**Verse:** 14
**Hebrew:** כִּי נֶפֶשׁ כָּל-בָּשָׂר דָּמוֹ בְנַפְשׁוֹ הוּא (*ki nefesh kol-basar damo be-nafsho hu'*) — repeated in v. 14
**EMET:** preserves the repetition in full
**LOGOS:** preserves the repetition in full
**PNEUMA:** "because the life of every creature is its blood" — compresses, does not repeat the formula
**Ruling:** Retain the full repetition of the formula
**Type:** Structural / completeness
**Notes:** The MT repeats the blood-life formula in v. 14 with slight variation — this is a deliberate rhetorical intensification. V. 14 states it twice: first as the basis for the prohibition ("for the life of every creature is its blood — its blood is its life"), then as the explanation for the prohibition's scope ("for the life of every creature is its blood"). PNEUMA's compression of the repetition into a single clause loses this intensification. The triple statement of the blood-life principle across vv. 11, 14a, and 14b is structural and is preserved.

---

## Disagreement 29 — V. 15: *nevalah* — "carcass" vs. "what has died naturally" vs. "animal that died on its own"

**Verse:** 15
**Hebrew:** נְבֵלָה (*nevalah*)
**EMET:** "a carcass" (with extended notes on the term)
**LOGOS:** "what has died naturally"
**PNEUMA:** "an animal that died on its own"
**Ruling:** "a carcass [*nevalah*]"
**Type:** Technical term / transliteration
**Notes:** *Nevalah* is a technical legal category (animal dead without ritual slaughter, blood not drained) foundational to later kashrut law. The consensus renders it "carcass" with the Hebrew term bracketed — following the project convention for technical terms at their first conjunction (cf. *se'irim*, *niddah*, *ketonet passim*). LOGOS's "what has died naturally" is accurate but definitional rather than terminological. PNEUMA's "animal that died on its own" is explanatory. The technical term deserves to be flagged with the Hebrew for the reader's awareness.

---

## Disagreement 30 — V. 15: *treifah* — "torn animal" vs. "what was torn by beasts" vs. "torn by wild beasts"

**Verse:** 15
**Hebrew:** טְרֵפָה (*treifah*)
**EMET:** "torn [animal]"
**LOGOS:** "what was torn by beasts"
**PNEUMA:** "torn by wild beasts"
**Ruling:** "a torn animal [*treifah*]"
**Type:** Technical term / transliteration
**Notes:** *Treifah* (from *taraf*, "to tear") is the foundational term for an animal mauled by a predator — the origin of the later Yiddish "treif" for all non-kosher food. At its first conjunction with *nevalah* in v. 15, the Hebrew term is retained in brackets per project convention. LOGOS's "what was torn by beasts" and PNEUMA's "torn by wild beasts" are definitionally accurate but lose the technical term. The ruling is "a torn animal [*treifah*]" — compact, precise, and flagging the Hebrew for readers.

---

## Disagreement 31 — V. 15: "native" vs. "native-born"

**Verse:** 15
**Hebrew:** הָאֶזְרָח (*ha-ezrach*)
**EMET:** "native"
**LOGOS:** "native"
**PNEUMA:** "native-born"
**Ruling:** "native"
**Type:** Word choice / legal formula
**Notes:** *Ha-ezrach* = "the native" (one who has sprung up in the land). "Native" is the consistent project rendering for *ezrach* throughout Leviticus (cf. Lev 16:29). PNEUMA's "native-born" adds "born" not present in the Hebrew. EMET and LOGOS agree; ruling follows them.

---

## Disagreement 32 — V. 16: *nasa' avono* — "bear his iniquity" vs. "bear the consequences of their sin"

**Verse:** 16
**Hebrew:** וְנָשָׂא עֲוֹנוֹ (*ve-nasa' avono*)
**EMET:** "he shall bear his iniquity"
**LOGOS:** "he shall bear his iniquity"
**PNEUMA:** "they shall bear the consequences of their sin"
**Ruling:** "he shall bear his iniquity"
**Type:** Technical formula / word choice
**Notes:** *Nasa' avon* = "to carry/bear iniquity" — the standard Levitical formula for personal accountability for unresolved guilt (cf. Lev 5:1, 17; 7:18; 17:16; 19:8; 20:17, 19; 22:16). PNEUMA introduces three departures: (1) shifts to the plural "they" (not in the Hebrew — the suffix is masculine singular *avono* = "his iniquity"); (2) adds "consequences of" (interpretive expansion not in the Hebrew); (3) replaces *avon* ("iniquity") with "sin" — *avon* and *chatah* are distinct categories in the Levitical vocabulary (*avon* = iniquity/guilt of a specific type, *chatah* = sin more generally). The technical formula is retained in the consensus, following EMET and LOGOS.

---

## Disagreement 33 — V. 6: LOGOS omits the *mishkan* / tabernacle clause from v. 4 structure

**Verse:** 4
**Type:** Structural note — already covered in Disagreement 9; logged separately for the record
**Notes:** LOGOS includes *lifnei mishkan YHWH* ("before the tabernacle of the LORD"). PNEUMA omits it. See Disagreement 9. This separate entry notes that EMET's explicit rendering of the phrase as "before the dwelling-place of the LORD" (*mishkan* = tabernacle/dwelling-place) vs. LOGOS's "before the tabernacle of the LORD" is a minor rendering difference: EMET: "dwelling-place" for *mishkan*; LOGOS: "tabernacle." Consensus: "tabernacle" — *mishkan* is the established "tabernacle" rendering throughout this translation series.

---

## Disagreement 34 — V. 8: PNEUMA shifts to second-person address ("among you")

**Verse:** 8
**Hebrew:** בְּתוֹכָם (*be-tokham*, "among them")
**EMET:** "among them"
**LOGOS:** "among them"
**PNEUMA:** "among you"
**Ruling:** "among them"
**Type:** Person shift
**Notes:** *Be-tokham* = "among them" (third person plural). In the speech structure of v. 8, Moses is instructed to "say to them" (*va-alehem tomar*), and the statement addresses "any man of the house of Israel... or of the sojourners who sojourn *among them*." PNEUMA shifts the pronoun to "among you," as if the reader is the addressee. This is consistent with PNEUMA's general tendency to address the reader directly, but it departs from the Hebrew third-person and from the speech structure. EMET and LOGOS are correct; ruling follows them.

---

## Disagreement 35 — V. 10: "that person who eats blood" — *nefesh* as "person"

**Verse:** 10
**Hebrew:** בַּנֶּפֶשׁ הָאֹכֶלֶת אֶת-הַדָּם (*ba-nefesh ha-okhelim et-ha-dam*)
**EMET:** "against the person who eats blood"
**LOGOS:** "against that person who eats blood"
**PNEUMA:** "against that person who eats blood"
**Ruling:** "against that person who eats blood"
**Type:** *nefesh* rendering
**Notes:** All three agents agree that *nefesh* here = "person" (the individual living being). No substantive disagreement; the ruling notes that this use of *nefesh* as "person" in v. 10 is the same word translated "life" in v. 11 — the irony EMET notes (the *nefesh* that eats blood destroys what blood represents) is theologically important and is preserved in the Council Notes.

---

## Disagreement 36 — V. 5: PNEUMA's sentence restructuring

**Verse:** 5
**EMET/LOGOS:** Preserve the MT's purpose structure: "This is in order that the sons of Israel may bring their sacrifices... and bring them to the LORD... and sacrifice them as peace offerings."
**PNEUMA:** "The purpose of this is so that the Israelites will bring to the LORD the animals they slaughter in the open country. They must bring them to the priest... and offer them as fellowship offerings."
**Ruling:** Follow EMET/LOGOS structure
**Type:** Structural / completeness
**Notes:** PNEUMA restructures v. 5 into two sentences and introduces "the animals they slaughter in the open country" (replacing "their sacrifices that they sacrifice upon the face of the open field"). PNEUMA's rendering dissolves the parallel structure: *et-zivcheihem asher hem zovechim al-penei ha-sadeh* = "their sacrifices which they are sacrificing upon the face of the open field" — the participial clause (*asher hem zovechim*) characterizes the sacrifices as those presently being offered in the wrong location. The formal structure is preserved in the consensus.

---

## Disagreement 37 — V. 7: PNEUMA adds "the spirits" as an interpretive insertion

**Verse:** 7
**Hebrew:** לַשְּׂעִירִם אֲשֶׁר הֵם זֹנִים אַחֲרֵיהֶם (*la-se'irim asher hem zonim achareihem*)
**EMET:** "to the goat-demons, after whom they are going a-whoring after them"
**LOGOS:** "to the goat-demons [se'irim], after whom they are playing the harlot"
**PNEUMA:** "to the goat-demons — the spirits to whom they have been prostituting themselves"
**Ruling:** "to the goat-demons [*se'irim*], after whom they are going a-whoring"
**Type:** Interpretive addition / word choice
**Notes:** PNEUMA adds "the spirits" as a gloss ("the goat-demons — the spirits to whom...") — an interpretive identification not present in the Hebrew. While accurate (the *se'irim* are demonic entities), this is an addition to the text. LOGOS's rendering is preferred: "to the goat-demons [*se'irim*]" with the Hebrew transliteration in brackets as the project's standard convention for technical terms. The bracketed transliteration serves the same function as PNEUMA's "the spirits" gloss without adding to the text itself.

---

## Disagreement 38 — V. 13: PNEUMA renders "they shall drain out its blood"

**Verse:** 13
**Hebrew:** וְשָׁפַךְ אֶת-דָּמוֹ (*ve-shafach et-damo*)
**EMET:** "he shall pour out its blood"
**LOGOS:** "he shall pour out its blood"
**PNEUMA:** "they shall drain out its blood"
**Ruling:** "he shall pour out its blood"
**Type:** Word choice / number
**Notes:** Two issues: (1) PNEUMA uses "drain," which implies a gradual emptying process (as through a drain), not the deliberate pouring-out action of *shafach* ("to pour, to shed, to spill"). "Pour out" is the correct rendering. (2) PNEUMA uses "they" (plural) where the Hebrew has a masculine singular subject (*ve-shafach* = "and he shall pour"). The regulation addresses the individual hunter. EMET and LOGOS agree on both "pour out" and "he." Ruling follows them.

---

## MT vs. LXX Divergence Summary

| Verse | MT | LXX Vaticanus | Impact on Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | *dam yechashev la-ish ha-hu'* ("bloodguilt shall be reckoned to that man") | αἷμα λογισθήσεται τῷ ἀνθρώπῳ ἐκείνῳ | Exact equivalence; *logizomai* = *chashav* — the imputation verb; no variant |
| 4 | *ve-nikrat ha-ish ha-hu' mi-qerev ammo* ("that man shall be cut off from among his people") | ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ἡ ψυχὴ ἐκείνη ἐκ τοῦ λαοῦ αὐτῆς | LXX intensifies to "utterly destroyed"; adds *psychē* where MT has *ha-ish*; LXX escalates penalty language |
| 5 | *zivchei shelamim* ("peace offerings") | θυσίαν σωτηρίου ("sacrifice of salvation/deliverance") | LXX uses *sōtērion* for *shelamim*, introducing soteriological overtone not in MT; consensus follows MT: "peace offerings" |
| 7 | *la-se'irim* ("to the goat-demons") | τοῖς ματαίοις ("to the vain/worthless things") | SIGNIFICANT: LXX demythologizes; eliminates demonic entities, renders as mere idols; consensus follows MT: "goat-demons" |
| 7 | *asher hem zonim achareihem* ("after whom they are going a-whoring") | οἷς αὐτοὶ ἐκπορνεύουσιν ὀπίσω αὐτῶν | LXX preserves the sexual-unfaithfulness metaphor with *ekporneuō*; functional equivalence |
| 10 | *ve-natatti panai ba-nefesh ha-okhelim et-ha-dam* ("I will set my face against that person who eats blood") | ἐπιστήσω τὸ πρόσωπόν μου ἐπὶ τὴν ψυχήν | LXX close; *epistēsō* ("I will set/direct") for *natatti* ("I will give/set") |
| 11 | *nefesh ha-basar ba-dam hi* ("the life of the flesh is in the blood") | ἡ γὰρ ψυχὴ πάσης σαρκὸς αἷμα αὐτοῦ ἐστίν ("the soul of all flesh — its blood is it") | LXX adds "all" (*pasēs*) anticipating v. 14; slightly different syntax; no material impact |
| 11 | *ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhapper* ("for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life") | τὸ γὰρ αἷμα αὐτοῦ ἀντὶ τῆς ψυχῆς ἐξιλάσεται ("for its blood — in exchange for the life — shall make atonement") | SIGNIFICANT: LXX adds *anti tēs psychēs* ("in exchange for the life") — making substitutionary logic explicit with *anti* ("in place of") where MT uses instrumental *be-* ("by means of"). LXX is interpretively correct but more explicit; consensus follows MT |
| 13 | *ve-kissahu be-afar* ("and shall cover it with dust") | καὶ καλύψει αὐτὸ χοῦν (χοῦν = "dust/earth") | LXX: *choun* ("dust/earth") — close equivalent; no variant |
| 15 | *nevalah o treifah* ("carcass or torn animal") | θνησιμαῖον ἢ θηριάλωτον | LXX: "died of itself" + "caught by a beast" — standard equivalents; no variant from MT meaning |
| 16 | *ve-nasa' avono* ("he shall bear his iniquity") | καὶ λήμψεται τὴν ἁμαρτίαν αὐτοῦ ("shall receive/take up his sin") | LXX: *lambanō* ("to take/receive") for *nasa'* ("to bear/carry"); *hamartian* ("sin") for *avon* ("iniquity") — slight semantic flattening; consensus follows MT: "bear his iniquity" |

---

*SOPHIA — Leviticus 17 disagreement log complete. 38 disagreements recorded.*
